mappings.rationaleAI: The Mughal Empire from Babur's founding through Aurangzeb's peak and 18th-century decline directly addresses the unification of the subcontinent under Mughal rule (C), including origins and conquest (C.1), Akbar's consolidation (C.2), the empire under Jahangir, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb (C.3), and decline after 1707 (C.4).

Akbar's military campaigns expanding Mughal dominion across Bihar, Bengal, Afghanistan, and Kashmir (C.2.a) and his administrative, fiscal, military, judicial, and religious policies including Din-i Ilahi (C.2.b) directly address the extension and consolidation of empire.
Timur's invasion of Delhi in 1398 directly relates to the Tughluq dynasty period, specifically the Mughal invasion and decline of Tughluq control (A.1.c).
Delhi as capital of the Delhi Sultanate (A.1) and the Mughal Empire (C), with UNESCO sites including Qutub Minar, Humayun's Tomb, and Red Fort, is directly relevant.
The Mongol invasion of 1398 under Timur relates to the Tughluq dynasty's decline following the Mughal invasion (A.1.c).

Comprehensive coverage of Delhi Sultanate, Mughal Empire, and regional kingdoms provides broad context across North India under Muslim hegemony (A), the Deccan and South India (B), Mughal unification (C), and the Maratha empire (D).
The early modern period's coverage of Mughal India provides context for the political and administrative unification of the subcontinent under the Mughal Empire (C).
Ibn Khaldun's historiographical methods and theories of dynastic cycles provide analytical context for cultural and religious developments during the Delhi sultanate (A.1.e).
Bangladesh's territory as part of Bengal under Sultanate and Mughal rule relates to the rise of Bengal as a regional kingdom (A.2) and Akbar's conquest of Bengal (C.2.a).
Pakistan's territory encompassing regions under Delhi Sultanate rule and Mughal administration provides context for North India under Muslim hegemony (A.1) and Mughal unification (C).
Portuguese exploration of the Indian Ocean relates to the arrival of the Portuguese in Ceylon and changes in foreign trade (E.1).
European colonial encroachment in South Asia relates to Mughal decline and foreign invasions in the 18th century (C.4).
The Black Death's spread through Central Asian trade networks intersects with the period of Tughluq dynasty decline and Mongol/Timurid invasions (A.1.c).
The Silk Road's trade integration with the Mughal Empire relates to the origins and commercial context of Mughal expansion (C.1).
